data.title: Gold Surges Above $5,000 an Ounce on Shutdown Fears, Geopolitical Tensions
data.description:
By Ronnie Harui


Gold prices climbed above the psychologically-important $5,000-per-ounce level for the first time, as investors sought the refuge of safe-haven assets on fears that the U.S. federal government may shut down for the second time in months.

Spot gold rose 1.2% to $5,049.68 a troy ounce on Monday after earlier touching a record high of $5,052.02 per ounce, ICE data showed. Spot silver also rose 3.8% to $107.22 an ounce after hitting a fresh all-time high of $107.30 per ounce earlier.

The risk of a government shutdown emerged as Senate Democrats, angered by the shooting in Minneapolis, said they wouldn't vote for a government funding package without major changes to the homeland security provisions. Lawmakers have to get a spending package to President Trump's desk for signature by Friday, or the shutdown could be triggered.

Also, Trump on his Truth Social platform Saturday warned that the U.S. would impose 100% tariffs on all Canadian goods and products coming into the U.S. if "Canada makes a deal with China." Trump's remarks threatened a major escalation in a brewing trade conflict with Canada.

"Precious metals show no signs of stopping on the upside," Sucden Financial's Research team said in commentary. "This momentum appears relentless, and for us, the question is not the directional view but how long market participants can finance these gains," the team added.

Precious metals have surged this year, driven by uncertainty in global economics and politics amid volatility in financial markets.

Some of those issues included the broad imposition of U.S. tariffs early last year, the U.S.'s seizure of Venezuela's strongman Nicolas Maduro, and increased concerns over the Federal Reserve's independence. More recently, President Trump's efforts to take control of Greenland sent the post-World War II alliance between the U.S. and its European partners into its worst crisis in over 70 years.

Spot gold has jumped around 17% in the year to date, while silver has powered roughly 50% higher, ICE data showed.


Write to Ronnie Harui at ronnie.harui@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-25-26 2017ET

Accidental 'crying horse' toy wins hearts in China
data.description:
YIWU, China, Jan 26 (Reuters) - At Yiwu International Trade City, China's largest wholesale market, customers crowd into a small shop searching for an unlikely bestseller ahead of the Lunar New Year.

They are looking for a red plush horse with a downturned mouth, a gold bell around its neck, and eyes that appear to shy away from a viewer's gaze. The toy has gone viral on Chinese social media ahead of the Spring Festival holiday, which this year marks the Year of the Horse in the Chinese zodiac.

Called the "crying horse" by online users, the toy was designed as a happy-faced Lunar New Year decoration, but a manufacturing mistake turned its smile into a frown.

"A worker sewed the mouth on upside down by accident," said Zhang Huoqing, owner of the Yiwu-based shop Happy Sister.

Zhang said she offered a refund after discovering the flaw, but the customer never returned the toy. Not long after, she discovered photos of it circulating online.

"People joked that the crying horse is how you look at work, while the smiling one is how you look after work," Zhang said. As demand surged, Zhang decided to keep making the sad-faced version.

Some young white-collar workers in China say the horse's dour expression mirrors their long hours and workplace stress.

It also taps into a broader trend for so-called "ugly-cute" toys, popularised in recent years by characters such as Pop Mart's toothy monster Labubu.

"These days, almost everyone who walks through the door asks for the crying horse," said Lou Zhenxian, a Yiwu vendor who has sold festive toys for more than 25 years.

By early afternoon, racks of crying horses outside Happy Sister had sold out and employees were rushing to restock the shelves.

"We will keep selling it," Zhang said. "This crying horse really fits the reality of modern working people."

(Reporting by Nicoco Chan and Chenxi Yang in Yiwu; Editing by Casey Hall and Tom Hogue)

data.title: Yen surges as intervention risks lurk, gold surpasses $5,000/oz
Tepco targets $20 billion in cost cuts between fiscal 2025-2034
Japan to closely coordinate with US on foreign exchange, top currency diplomat says
data.description:
TOKYO, Jan 26 (Reuters) - Japan's top currency diplomat Atsushi Mimura said on Monday the government will maintain close coordination with the United States on foreign exchange and act appropriately, while declining to confirm news reports of rate checks.

The yen spiked on Friday as the New York Federal Reserve conducted rate checks, heightening the chance of joint U.S.-Japan intervention to halt the currency's slide.

"We will continue to closely coordinate with the U.S. authorities as needed, based on a joint Japan-U.S. statement issued in September last year, and will respond appropriately," Mimura told reporters.

The September statement reaffirmed both countries' commitment to market-determined exchange rates while agreeing that foreign-exchange intervention should be reserved for combating excessive volatility.

Japanese officials have said it marked the first written U.S. confirmation of the right to intervene in the event of excessive volatility.

Mimura did not comment on the possibility of a coordinated market intervention by the two governments.

Japan's Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama also declined to comment on Fridays' reported rate checks that led to the yen suddenly jumping against the U.S. dollar.

"There is nothing I can talk about," Katayama said.

(Reporting by Makiko Yamazaki; Writing by Kantaro Komiya; Editing by Christopher Cushing and Shr Navaratnam)

By Makiko Yamazaki

Seven killed, scores missing as passenger ferry capsizes off southern Philippines
data.description:
MANILA, Jan 26 (Reuters) - At least seven people died after a passenger boat carrying more than 300 people capsized off the southern Philippine province of Basilan, the Philippine Coast Guard said.

The accident occurred after midnight on Monday as the passenger vessel, MV Trisha Kerstin 3, was en route to Jolo in Sulu province, after departing Zamboanga. The PCG said the vessel had 332 passengers on record and 27 crew.

Coast Guard Commander Romel Dua of Southern Mindanao District told DZBB radio that 215 people had been rescued and seven bodies recovered, while search and rescue operations continued for 144 others who remained missing.

Dua said an investigation was underway to determine the cause of the accident.

Basilan Governor Mujiv Hataman shared on Facebook clips from the scene at Isabela port in Mindanao which showed survivors being ushered off boats, some wrapped in thermal blankets, and others being carried on stretchers.

Scores of people die each year from ferry accidents in the Philippines, an archipelago of more than 7,000 islands with a patchy record for maritime safety.

(Reporting by Karen Lema; Editing by Michael Perry)

data.title: Gold rushes past $5,000 to record high on safe-haven rush
data.description:
Jan 26 (Reuters) - Gold surged to a record high above $5,000 an ounce on Monday, extending a historic rally as investors piled into the safe-haven asset amid rising geopolitical tensions.

Spot gold rose 0.85% to $5,024.95 per ounce by 2341 GMT, while U.S. gold futures for February delivery gained 0.91% to $5,024.60 per ounce.

Gold soared 64% in 2025, underpinned by sustained safe-haven demand, U.S. monetary policy easing, robust central bank purchases - with China extending its gold-buying spree for a fourteenth month in December - and record inflows into exchange-traded funds. Prices have gained more than 16% this year.

"We expect further upside. Our current forecast suggests that prices will peak at around $5,500 later this year," said Philip Newman, director at Metals Focus.

"Periodic pullbacks are likely as investors take profits, but we expect each correction to be short-lived and met with strong buying interest," Newman added.

Escalating friction between the United States and NATO over Greenland has added fresh momentum to gold's advance this year on expectations of more financial and geopolitical uncertainty.

On the geopolitical front, Ukraine and Russia ended a second day of U.S.-brokered talks in Abu Dhabi on Saturday without a deal, with more discussions expected next weekend, even as overnight Russian airstrikes knocked out power for over a million Ukrainians amid subzero cold.

Adding to the uncertainty, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Saturday he would impose a 100% tariff on Canada if it follows through on a trade deal with China, warning Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney that a deal would endanger his country.

"Our forecast for the year is that gold will see a high of $6,400 an ounce with an average of $5,375," independent analyst Ross Norman said.

Spot silver rose 1.72% to $104.72 per ounce. Spot platinum was steady at $2,767 per ounce, while spot palladium rose 0.17% to $2,013.50 per ounce.

Silver climbed above the $100 mark for the first time on Friday, building on its 147% rise last year as retail-investor flows and momentum-driven buying compounded a prolonged spell of tightness in physical markets for the metal.

(Reporting by Anjana Anil and Kavya Balaraman in Bengaluru; Additional reporting by Pablo Sinha; Himani Sarkar and Subhranshu Sahu)

By Kavya Balaraman and Anjana Anil

Noda: Govt, BOJ should tweak current joint agreement focusing on beating deflation to one clarifying role each would play in taming inflation
Japan ex-finance minister and head of largest opposition party Cra Noda: currency intervention likely to have limited effect in sustainably halting yen slide
Noda: Japan must halt excessive yen falls with stronger message to markets of its resolve to get fiscal house in order
Week Ahead for FX, Bonds : Fed Rate Decision in Focus
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires staff


Below are the most important global events likely to affect FX and bond markets in the week starting Jan. 26.

Following a turbulent week that ended with calm after President Trump backed off his threats to impose tariffs against European countries over Greenland, focus returns to economics and monetary policy, with a Federal Reserve meeting likely to be the highlight of the days to come.

Investors will be watching for clues on the likely timing of the next interest-rate cut as data show signs that the U.S. economy is improving.

The Bank of Canada will announce an interest-rate decision, as will central banks in Singapore, Sweden, Brazil and South Africa.

In Asia, focus will center on China factory activity data, central bank minutes from Japan as volatility in the yen and in Japanese government bonds grab investors' attention, plus Australian inflation data.


U.S.


The Federal Reserve is widely expected to keep the federal funds target range unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% when it announces a decision on Wednesday.

Focus will center on how many dissenting votes there are and on any accompanying commentary as investors gauge the likely timing and pace of further interest-rate reductions.

Recent data have shown the U.S. economy performing better than expected, although concerns remain about a weak labor market, while inflation has continued to edge lower from elevated levels.

"We expect Fed Chair Jerome Powell to emphasize that future rate moves will depend on how the economic data evolve," HSBC economists said in a note.

U.S. money markets currently fully price a next 25 basis-point rate reduction in July, with a sizeable chance of another rate cut by year-end, LSEG data show.

Investors will meanwhile continue to watch out for any news about President Trump's favored choice to replace Powell when his term ends in May, as well as any potential Supreme Court ruling on the legality of Trump's tariffs.

Economic data in the coming week include durable goods figures for November on Monday; the Conference Board's consumer confidence survey for January and the November Case-Shiller house price index on Tuesday; and weekly jobless claims, November trade figures and November factory orders on Thursday. Friday will see the release of December producer prices figures for a snapshot of pipeline inflationary pressures.

The U.S. Treasury will auction two-year notes on Monday, five-year notes on Tuesday and seven-year notes on Thursday.


Canada


The Bank of Canada announces a decision on Wednesday and is widely expected to keep interest rates on hold at 2.25%.

Focus will center on accompanying commentary on the inflation outlook and on the impact of geopolitical and trade uncertainties as investors gauge whether the central bank could potentially reduce rates again in the coming months.

Analysts at Bank of America expect the BOC will resume cutting interest rates in April and June as core inflation decelerates.

Canadian money markets price in only an 18% chance that the central bank will cut interest rates by another 25 basis points by June. They then price a risk of a rate hike by the end of the year, LSEG data show.

Canadian trade data for November are released Thursday, followed by gross domestic product data for November on Friday.


Latin America


The next interest rate decision from Brazil's central bank is due on Wednesday.

LSEG data show markets price a 95% chance that rates will be left unchanged at 15%. However, Brazil's inflation eased to 4.3% in December from 4.5% in November, and this leaves Tuesday's decision "finely balanced" with the chance of a rate cut, Capital Economics economist Kimberley Sperrfechter said in a note.

"Whether the first cut in the cycle comes this month or in March, rates are likely to come down further than is widely anticipated."

Chile is expected to reduce interest rates in a decision on Tuesday, while Colombia's central bank is expected to hike rates when it announces a decision on Friday.


Eurozone


Germany's Ifo business climate index for January on Monday will kick off a week busy for eurozone data. France will release a consumer confidence survey for January on Tuesday, followed by Germany's GfK consumer climate survey on Wednesday, alongside Italy's business and consumer confidence surveys.

Spain will release fourth-quarter unemployment data on Tuesday and retail sales figures for December on Thursday.

Flash-estimate gross domestic product data for the fourth quarter from France, Spain, Germany, Italy and the eurozone are due Friday. On the same day, flash-estimate CPI inflation data for January from Spain and Germany are released, alongside German unemployment data for January. Italian and eurozone unemployment data as well as French consumer spending data are also due Friday.

With a large number of syndicated bond issues year to date, the syndication pipeline looks set to thin out near term, leaving focus on auctions. Germany will launch a new March 2028 Schatz on Tuesday and will reopen the February 2036 Bund on Wednesday, each for 6 billion euros. Italy will conduct end-of-month auctions on Tuesday and Thursday.


U.K.


Consumer credit, mortgage lending and mortgage approvals data for December are released by the Bank of England on Friday.

Investec economist Ellie Henderson said November's lending data suggested the U.K. housing market was "fairly resilient in the second half of last year" and 2025 is unlikely to have ended on a sour note.

Nationwide's house price index for January is also released Friday, while the British Retail Consortium's January shop price index is released Tuesday.

The U.K. is due to sell March 2033 gilts via auction on Tuesday.


Scandinavia


Sweden's Riksbank announces a policy decision on Thursday and is expected to leave its key policy rate unchanged at 1.75%, even after recent data showed core inflation declined more than expected in December.

SEB economists said the decision comes against a backdrop of "surprisingly low inflation, a weak labor market but also a solid pick-up in growth." December meeting minutes also suggested that the Riksbank's governor is "setting a high bar for near-term changes to the policy rate."

"It is very likely that the Board will reiterate the message of unchanged rates at least until the end of 2026 on January 26," they said.

SEB expects rates to stay on hold in 2026.

Swedish gross domestic product data for December and the fourth quarter are also released on Thursday.

Sweden and Norway will hold bond auctions on Wednesday.


Hungary


Hungary's central bank announces a rate decision on Tuesday, where it is widely expected to leave interest rates on hold at 6.50%.

The central bank opened the door to interest-rate cuts at its last meeting, though the chances of a rate reduction this month are "pretty much non-existent," ING analyst Peter Virovacz said in a note.

"The recent inflation figures and their composition came as an unpleasant surprise to the central bank. Geopolitical upheaval and market aversion also do not bode well for a rate cut," he said.


South Africa


The South African Reserve Bank announces a rate decision on Thursday.

Economists at HSBC expect the central bank to cut the policy rate by 25 basis points to 6.50% due to reduced price pressures and a "notably stronger-than-expected" South African rand.

A significant decline in inflation expectations in the fourth quarter of last year, sustained precious metal price gains and a policy rate that remains in restrictive territory "should provide sufficient justification for a frontloaded cut," they said.


Japan


Japanese government data due at the end of the week is expected to show that consumer price growth for the Tokyo metropolitan area remained above the Bank of Japan's 2% target in January, though the pace of inflation may have slowed amid falling energy prices.

Industrial production, retail sales and jobless rates for December are also scheduled for release on Friday.

On Wednesday, the Bank of Japan will release the minutes of its meeting in December, when it decided to raise interest rates to 0.75%--its highest level in three decades.

The Bank of Japan is scheduled to make outright purchases of four sectors of Japan's government bond market on Thursday, including sovereign securities with tenors of more than 5 years and up to 10 years and those with tenors of more than 10 years and up to 25 years. The planned purchases are expected to support the domestic bond market.

The Ministry of Finance is scheduled to hold three auctions during the week which may be of interest to investors. It will sell about 300 billion yen of 5-year climate transition debt on Monday, around 400 billion yen of 40-year bonds on Wednesday, and about 2.8 trillion yen of 2-year debt on Friday. The 40-year JGBs to be issued in January will be a reopening of the May 2025 issue, according to the ministry.


China


This week, investors are turning their attention to China's latest industrial profit data for December, due on Tuesday, amid fierce price wars that have driven corporate profits lower and prompted authorities to monitor closely.

ING economists expect industrial profits to have improved last month despite continued pressure across many sectors, with a modest pickup in growth momentum in the second half of 2025 potentially lifting full-year profit growth into slightly positive territory.

Beijing is also set to release January's manufacturing and non-manufacturing purchasing managers index readings on Saturday.

Citi economists project manufacturing PMI to remain at 50.1, unchanged from last month. They expect seasonal effects and an unfavorable base to modestly weigh on January's data, even though Lunar New Year falls in February this year. The cold weather in mid-January also caused temporary supply-side disruptions, though export orders should be still resilient, which is keeping manufacturing demand stable this month.


Australia


(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

01-25-26 1914ET
